contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How HTTP communication works (requests and responses)
- The differences between [[HTTP]] [[methods|method]] (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
- The meaning of **status codes** (200, 404, 500, etc.)

Let's understand the fundamentals of HTTP communication, which forms the foundation of web development.

contents.title: How Web Applications Work
contents.body:
Web communication follows this flow:

1. The client (browser) sends a request to the server
2. The server processes the request
3. The server returns a response
4. The browser displays the response

The communication protocol for this is HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol).

URL structure:
  https://example.com:8080/api/users?name=taro
  ├─ Protocol (https)
  ├─ Hostname (example.com)
  ├─ Port number (8080)
  ├─ Path (/api/users)
  └─ Query parameter (name=taro)
contents.note: HTTP is a stateless protocol (it does not maintain state). Each request-response pair is independent, and the server does not remember previous communications.

contents.title: HTTP Methods
contents.body:
HTTP requests include a method that indicates "what you want to do."

| Method | Purpose | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| GET | Retrieve data | Parameters are included in the URL (?name=taro&age=25) |
| POST | Send data (form submission, login) | Parameters are in the request body. Can send large amounts of data |
| PUT | Update data (overwrite existing resources) | Used in REST APIs |
| DELETE | Delete data | Used in REST APIs |
| PATCH | Partially update data | Used in REST APIs |

GET and POST are the basics of web apps. PUT/DELETE/PATCH are used in REST APIs.

When to use GET vs POST:
- GET: For retrieval. Can be bookmarked. Saved in browser history
- POST: For submission. Use POST for sensitive data like passwords
contents.note: GET request parameters are exposed in the URL, so never send confidential information like passwords via GET.

contents.title: HTTP Status Codes
contents.body:
Responses from the server include a **status code** indicating the result of processing.

**[2xx Success]**
| Code | Description |
|---|---|
| 200 OK | Processing completed successfully |
| 201 Created | New resource created successfully |
| 204 No Content | Processing succeeded (no data to return) |

**[3xx Redirect]**
| Code | Description |
|---|---|
| 301 Moved Permanently | Permanent relocation |
| 302 Found | Temporary redirect |

**[4xx Client Error]**
| Code | Description |
|---|---|
| 400 Bad Request | Invalid request |
| 401 Unauthorized | Authentication required |
| 403 Forbidden | Access denied |
| 404 Not Found | Resource not found |
| 405 Method Not Allowed | HTTP method not permitted |

**[5xx Server Error]**
| Code | Description |
|---|---|
| 500 Internal Server Error | Internal server error |
| 503 Service Unavailable | Service temporarily unavailable |
contents.note: Remember: 404 means "page not found" and 500 means "server bug." If you get a 500 error during development, check the server logs.

contents.title: HTTP Communication in Java (HttpClient)
contents.body: Since Java 11, you can perform HTTP communication using the standard library's HttpClient.
contents.note: HttpClient was officially introduced in Java 11. Before that, HttpURLConnection or Apache HttpClient was used.

contents.title: HTTP Methods and CRUD Correspondence
contents.body:
HTTP methods express "what you want to do."

| HTTP Method | Purpose | CRUD | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| GET | Retrieve | Read | Get user info |
| POST | Create | Create | Register new user |
| PUT | Update | Update | Update profile |
| DELETE | Delete | Delete | Delete account |

You can access GET by typing a URL directly in the address bar, but POST is used from form submissions or APIs.
contents.note: **Status codes** 2xx=Success, 3xx=Redirect, 4xx=Client error, 5xx=Server error

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Web communication is fundamentally a single request → response round trip
- HTTP methods: GET (retrieve), POST (submit), PUT (update), DELETE (delete)
- GET parameters are exposed in the URL; POST parameters are stored in the body
- **Status codes**: 2xx=Success, 4xx=Client error, 5xx=Server error
- Java 11's HttpClient enables HTTP communication using only standard libraries
- HTTP is a stateless protocol (does not maintain state)

quizzes.question: When submitting a password from a user login form, which is the appropriate HTTP method?
quizzes.explanation: Sensitive information such as passwords should be sent using the POST method. With the GET method, parameters are included in the URL, which means passwords could remain in browser history or server logs. With POST, data is stored in the request body and is not displayed in the URL.

quizzes.question: What does HTTP status code 404 mean?
quizzes.explanation: 404 Not Found indicates that the requested resource (page or API) does not exist on the server. It is returned when accessing a mistyped URL or a deleted page. 500 is an internal server error, 401 is an authentication error, and 301 is a redirect.

quizzes.question: Arrange the steps of web communication in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: Web communication follows this flow: the client (browser) sends a request, the server receives and processes it, returns a response, and finally the browser displays it. HTTP is a stateless protocol that completes in a single round trip.

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basic structure of **servlets** (HttpServlet)
- Implementing doGet() and doPost() [[methods|method]]
- How to retrieve request parameters

[[Servlets|Servlet]] are the foundational technology for Java web applications.

contents.title: How Servlets Work
contents.body:
**Servlet** operation flow:

1. Client sends an HTTP request
2. The web container (Tomcat, etc.) receives the request
3. Calls the appropriate method of the servlet [[class]]
   → GET request → doGet() method
   → POST request → doPost() method
4. The servlet processes and generates a response
5. The web container sends the response to the client

Key components:
| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| HttpServlet | Base class for servlets. Extend this class |
| HttpServletRequest | Request information (parameters, headers, etc.) |
| HttpServletResponse | Response generation (HTML output, status codes, etc.) |
| Web Container | Servlet execution environment (Apache Tomcat, Jetty, etc.) |
contents.note: The web container (**servlet** container) manages servlet lifecycle, [[thread]] management, and request routing.

contents.title: Basic Servlet Implementation
contents.body: [[Inherit|Inheritance]] from HttpServlet and [[override]] the doGet/doPost methods.
contents.note: The @WebServlet annotation specifies the URL pattern. Previously, this was configured in web.xml, but since Java EE 6, annotations have become the standard approach.

contents.title: Servlet Lifecycle
contents.body:
Let's understand the lifespan of a **servlet**.

| Timing | Method Called | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Servlet initialization | init() | Only once |
| Each request | doGet()/doPost() | Every time |
| Servlet destruction | destroy() | Only once |

Typically, only one [[instance]] of a servlet is created and shared across multiple requests.
contents.note: Therefore, you should not store request-specific data in **servlet** fields (not thread-safe).

contents.title: Processing Form Data
contents.body: An example of receiving data submitted from an HTML form in a **servlet**.
contents.note: To prevent character encoding issues, call req.setCharacterEncoding("UTF-8") first.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Servlets** are created by extending HttpServlet
- doGet() handles GET requests, doPost() handles POST requests
- The @WebServlet annotation specifies the URL pattern
- getParameter() retrieves request parameters
- setContentType() sets the MIME type of the response
- A web container (Tomcat, etc.) provides the servlet execution environment

quizzes.question:
Find the error in the following servlet code.
This code has a compile error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: res.getWriter() may throw an IOException, but there is no throws declaration or try-catch. The correct signature should be `protected void doPost(...) throws ServletException, IOException`. This is also needed to match the signature of HttpServlet's doPost method.

choices.text: Missing throws IOException, no exception handling for getWriter()
The argument to getParameter is incorrect
The format of setContentType is wrong

quizzes.question:
Fill in the blank to get the value of the request parameter "name".
String name = req._____("name");
quizzes.explanation: The getParameter() method retrieves request parameters. For GET requests, it retrieves from the URL query string; for POST requests, from the request body. If the parameter does not exist, null is returned.

quizzes.question: Which software provides the execution environment for servlets?
quizzes.explanation: Apache Tomcat is a Java web container (servlet container) that provides the execution environment for servlets. It manages servlet lifecycle, request routing, thread management, etc. MySQL is a database, Maven is a build tool, and Git is a version control system.

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The stateless nature of HTTP and the need for sessions
- How to manage login state with HttpSession
- How cookies work and how to use them

Learn the mechanisms for identifying users and maintaining state.

contents.title: Differences Between Cookies and Sessions
contents.body:
| Item | Cookie | Session |
|---|---|---|
| Storage location | Client-side (browser) | Server-side |
| Data size | Up to about 4KB | Can handle large data |
| Security | Users can delete/disable | Data is not sent to the client |
| Expiration | Configurable | Automatically deleted on timeout |
| Identification | − | Session ID sent/received via cookies |

How it works:
  1. On the first access, the server issues a session ID
  2. The session ID is sent to the browser via a cookie
  3. On the next access, the browser sends the session ID back
  4. The server retrieves the user's data using the session ID
contents.note: The session data is stored on the server, and only the session ID is saved in the cookie. This is how "HTTP sessions" work.

contents.title: Using HttpSession
contents.body: An example of managing login state using sessions in a **servlet**.
contents.note: getSession(false) returns the existing session only if one exists. getSession() or getSession(true) creates a new session if one doesn't exist.

contents.title: Differences Between Sessions and Cookies
contents.body:
Both are used for state management, but they differ in storage location and usage.

| Item | Session | Cookie |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Server-side | Browser-side |
| Data size | Unlimited | About 4KB |
| Security | Secure | Can be tampered with on client-side |
| Expiration | Server-configured | Freely configurable |

Sessions are commonly used for login state, while cookies are often used for "remember me" functionality.

contents.title: Working with Cookies
contents.body: How to manipulate cookies directly from a **servlet**.
contents.note: setHttpOnly(true) is important for XSS attack prevention. It prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie, improving security.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- HTTP is stateless → Sessions/Cookies maintain state
- Cookie: Stores small data on the client-side (4KB limit)
- Session: Stores data on the server-side (higher security)
- The session ID is stored in a cookie and linked to server data
- getSession() retrieves the session; setAttribute/getAttribute for data operations
- setHttpOnly(true) for XSS prevention
- Sessions are automatically invalidated by timeout

quizzes.question: Where is HttpSession data stored?
quizzes.explanation: HttpSession data is stored in the server's memory. Only a session ID is sent to the client (browser) as a cookie for identification purposes. This ensures security, preventing confidential data such as passwords and personal information from being sent to the client.

quizzes.question: Predict the content displayed in the browser on the first access for the following servlet code.
quizzes.explanation: getSession(false) returns the existing session only if one exists; otherwise, it returns null. On first access, no session exists, so session==null, and "Guest" is displayed. Understanding the difference from getSession(true) or getSession() is important.

quizzes.question: What is the effect of setting setHttpOnly(true) on a Cookie?
quizzes.explanation: Setting setHttpOnly(true) prevents JavaScript from accessing that cookie. This is important for XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attack prevention. It prevents attackers from stealing session IDs by reading document.cookie via JavaScript.

choices.text: Cookies are only sent over HTTPS

quizzes.question: Complete the HttpServlet GET request handler.
quizzes.explanation: Extend HttpServlet using extends and override doGet() to handle GET requests.

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Embedding dynamic values in HTML with JSP (JavaServer Pages)
- The concept of the MVC pattern (Model-View-Controller)
- The division of responsibilities between **servlets** and JSP

This is a design pattern for building maintainable web applications.

contents.title: Basic JSP Syntax
contents.body: JSP files (.jsp) are template engines that allow embedding Java code within HTML.
contents.code:
<%-- userList.jsp --%> <%@ page contentType="text/html; charset=UTF-8" %> <%@ page import="java.util.List" %>

User List

<%-- Scriptlet: Embedding Java code --%> <% List users = (List) request.getAttribute("users"); %> <% for (String user : users) { %> <%-- Expression: Output a value --%> <% } %>
Name
<%= user %>
<%-- EL (Expression Language): A more concise syntax --%>

Number of users: ${users.size()}

Request parameter: ${param.name}
contents.note: In modern JSP development, scriptlets (<% %>) are no longer recommended. Using EL expressions (${...}) and JSTL tag libraries is the preferred approach.

contents.title: MVC Pattern Role Division
contents.body:
MVC is a fundamental design pattern for web applications.

| Component | Role | Java Web Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| Model (M) | Data and business logic | JavaBeans, Service |
| View (V) | Screen display | JSP |
| Controller (C) | Request handling and dispatch | Servlet |

By separating responsibilities, changes to the UI or logic can be made independently.
contents.note: In Spring MVC, this pattern is used in an even more advanced form (see Chapter 38).

contents.title: MVC Pattern
contents.body:
MVC (Model-View-Controller) is a design pattern for web applications.

| Component | Role | Java Implementation Example |
|---|---|---|
| Model | Data and business logic | POJO, DAO, Service (UserDAO, OrderService) |
| View | Screen display | JSP, Thymeleaf, HTML templates |
| Controller | Request processing control | Servlet, @Controller |

Benefits of MVC:
1. Separation of concerns: UI, logic, and control are independent
2. Maintainability: UI changes don't affect logic
3. Testability: Models can be unit tested
4. Team development: Front-end and back-end teams can work in parallel
contents.note: In [[Spring Boot]], the MVC pattern is applied as standard. @Controller takes the role of **servlets**, and Thymeleaf etc. serves as the View instead of JSP.

contents.title: MVC Implementation with Servlet + JSP
contents.body: A pattern for passing data from a **servlet** (Controller) to a JSP (View).
contents.note: It is best practice to place JSP files under /WEB-INF/. WEB-INF is not directly accessible from the browser, so it forces requests to go through a **servlet**.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- JSP is a template that allows embedding Java code within HTML
- Using EL expressions (${...}) to replace scriptlets is the current recommendation
- MVC pattern: Model (data) + View (display) + Controller (control)
- Data is transferred from **servlet** (C) to JSP (V) via Forward
- JSP files should be placed under WEB-INF to prevent direct access
- Understand the differences between Forward and Redirect

quizzes.question: In the MVC pattern, what role does a servlet play?
quizzes.explanation: A servlet serves as the Controller in MVC. It handles request reception, invokes business logic (Model), and controls transfer to the view (View=JSP). The Model is handled by POJOs and DAOs, and the View is handled by JSP or templates.

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between forward and redirect?
quizzes.explanation: Forward delegates processing to another resource (such as JSP) internally on the server, so the browser URL does not change. Redirect instructs the browser to "re-access a different URL," so the URL changes. With forward, request Attributes can be carried over, but with redirect, they cannot.

choices.text: Forward changes the URL, redirect does not
Forward does not change the URL, redirect changes the URL
There is no difference between forward and redirect
POST data can be carried over with redirect

quizzes.question:
Fill in the blank for the JSP Expression Language (EL) syntax to display the value of the request attribute "title".
_____
quizzes.explanation: In EL (Expression Language), ${attributeName} outputs the value of a request scope attribute. Writing ${title} displays the value of request.getAttribute("title"). It is a more concise and safer syntax than scriptlets (<%= ... %>).

lessons.title: REST API Basics
lessons.summary: JSON communication and RESTful design concepts

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- **REST API** design principles (resource-oriented)
- Data exchange in JSON format
- The correspondence between CRUD and HTTP methods

Learn the basics of API development, the mainstream approach for modern web applications.

contents.title: RESTful Design Principles
contents.body:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an API design style. It follows these principles:

1. Resource-oriented: URLs represent resources (nouns)
   ○ /api/users (user list)
   ○ /api/users/1 (user with ID:1)
   × /api/getUser?id=1 (verbs are not recommended)

2. HTTP methods represent operations (see table below)

3. Stateless: The server does not maintain session state
   → Authentication uses tokens (JWT, etc.) included in requests

4. Appropriate **status codes**: Return 200, 201, 404, 500, etc. correctly

| HTTP Method + Path | Operation |
|---|---|
| GET /api/users | Get user list |
| GET /api/users/1 | Get user with ID:1 |
| POST /api/users | Create new user |
| PUT /api/users/1 | Update user with ID:1 |
| DELETE /api/users/1 | Delete user with ID:1 |
contents.note: **REST API** CRUD operations naturally correspond to HTTP methods. POST=Create, GET=Read, PUT=Update, DELETE=Delete.

contents.title: Reading and Writing JSON (Gson/Jackson)
contents.body: Java object ↔ JSON conversion uses dedicated libraries.
contents.note: Gson is developed by Google, and Jackson is developed by FasterXML. In [[Spring Boot]], Jackson is included by default.

contents.title: RESTful API Design Rules
contents.body:
Good **REST API** design follows certain rules.

- URLs represent resources (nouns): /users, /orders
- Verbs are expressed via HTTP methods: GET /users (list retrieval), POST /users (create new)
- Specify a specific resource by ID: GET /users/123
- JSON format is the mainstream for responses

Avoid URLs like "/getUser" or "/deleteUser."

contents.title: Implementing a REST API with Servlets
contents.body: An example of implementing a JSON-based **REST API** with **servlets**.
contents.note: With Spring Boot, using @RestController greatly simplifies this kind of code. You will learn about it in ch38.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **REST API** is a web API design style that exchanges data in JSON
- URLs represent resources (nouns), HTTP methods represent operations
- GET=Retrieve, POST=Create, PUT=Update, DELETE=Delete
- Content-Type: application/json for JSON communication
- Gson / Jackson for Java ↔ JSON conversion
- Return appropriate **status codes** (200, 201, 404, etc.)
- Spring Boot allows writing this pattern more concisely

quizzes.question: In RESTful API design, what is the correct request to "delete user with ID:5"?
quizzes.explanation: In REST APIs, the URL specifies the resource and the HTTP method expresses the operation. "/api/users/5" identifies the resource, and the DELETE method expresses deletion. Including verbs (deleteUser, etc.) in the URL goes against RESTful design.

quizzes.question: When a new resource has been successfully created, which HTTP status code should a REST API return?
quizzes.explanation: 201 Created is a status code indicating that a new resource was successfully created. It is returned when creating a new user via POST /api/users. 200 OK is for general success, and 204 No Content is for success without a response body.

quizzes.question:
When making a GET /api/items?category=book request to the following REST API, what is the HTTP status code of the response?
* Assume service.findByCategory("book") returns an empty List.
quizzes.explanation: Since findByCategory("book") returns an empty List, items.isEmpty() is true, and ResponseEntity.noContent().build() is executed. noContent() returns HTTP status code **204 No Content**. 200 is for success, 201 is for creation, and 404 is for not found.

lessons.title: Filters and Practical Web Design
lessons.summary: Centralizing common processing and overall web application design

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Centralizing common processing with Filters
- Event-driven processing with Listeners
- Practical design patterns for web development

Learn tips for designing maintainable applications as they grow in scale.

contents.title: Implementing a Filter
contents.body: Filters intercept processing before and after a request reaches a **servlet**.
contents.note: If you don't call chain.doFilter(), the request will not reach the **servlet**. In authentication filters, access control is achieved by not calling chain.doFilter() for unauthenticated users and redirecting instead.

contents.title: Filter Execution Order Follows web.xml Declaration Order
contents.body:
When multiple filters are defined, they execute in the order declared in web.xml (top to bottom).

Request: Filter A → Filter B → **Servlet**
Response: [[Servlet]] → Filter B → Filter A

Filters can implement both "pre-processing" and "post-processing" by writing logic before and after chain.doFilter().

contents.title: Implementing an Authentication Filter
contents.body: An example of checking whether a user is logged in using a filter.
contents.note: Multiple filters can be defined, and chain.doFilter() passes processing to the next filter. The order of @WebFilter is not guaranteed, so if order control is needed, define them in web.xml.

contents.title: Web Application Project Structure
contents.body:
Standard directory structure for a Java web application (Maven):

src/main/
├── java/com/example/
│   ├── servlet/        ← Controller (**servlets**)
│   │   ├── UserListServlet.java
│   │   └── LoginServlet.java
│   ├── service/        ← Business logic
│   │   └── UserService.java
│   ├── dao/            ← Data access (DB operations)
│   │   └── UserDAO.java
│   ├── model/          ← Data model (POJO)
│   │   └── User.java
│   └── filter/         ← Filters
│       └── AuthFilter.java
├── resources/          ← Configuration files
└── webapp/
    ├── WEB-INF/
    │   ├── web.xml     ← Deployment descriptor
    │   └── views/      ← JSP files
    └── css/js/images/  ← Static resources

This structure allows organization aligned with the MVC pattern.
contents.note: In [[Spring Boot]], the traditional web.xml is not needed, and web application setup is automated through AutoConfiguration. However, understanding Servlet specifications for conceptual knowledge is very important.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Filters intercept common processing before and after requests/responses
- @WebFilter specifies the URL pattern to apply the filter
- chain.doFilter() passes processing to the next filter or **servlet**
- Used for cross-cutting concerns like authentication checks, logging, and character encoding
- Not calling chain.doFilter() blocks the request
- MVC + Filters enable building maintainable web applications
- Spring Boot internally uses Servlet/Filter technology

quizzes.question: Find the issue in the following authentication filter code where authenticated user requests do not reach the servlet.
quizzes.explanation: Even when authenticated, **chain.doFilter(req, res) is not called**, so the request does not reach the servlet. chain.doFilter() is the method that passes the request to the next filter or servlet. Forgetting to call it causes the request to stop.

choices.text: chain.doFilter() is not called in the authenticated branch
The argument to sendRedirect is wrong
getSession(true) should be used instead of getSession(false)

quizzes.question: Which layer of a web application is responsible for database access?
quizzes.explanation: The DAO (Data Access Object) layer is responsible for database access. It handles executing SQL, retrieving results, and converting to data models. The Service layer handles business logic, Controller (servlet) handles request processing, and View (JSP) handles screen display.

quizzes.question: Arrange the order in which a request is processed in a web application correctly.
quizzes.explanation: A request is first received by the web container (Tomcat, etc.), then passes through Filters (common processing like authentication/logging), is processed by the servlet (Controller), and finally the screen is generated by JSP (View). This flow is the foundation of the MVC + Filter pattern.

choices.text: JSP generates the view and returns the response
